mn64:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn64:10-12.1: Furthermore, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, with the stilling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*] … enters and dwells in the second *jhāna* …
mn64:10-12.3: he enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*.
mn64:10-12.4: Whatever there is there that is included in *rūpa*, included in *vedanā*, included in *saññā*, included in *saṅkhāra*, included in consciousness [*viññāṇagataṁ*] …
mn64:10-12.6: not liable to return from that world.
mn64:10-12.7: This too, Ānanda, is the path, this is the practice, for the abandoning of the five lower fetters.
mn64:13.1: Furthermore, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, with the complete transcending of perceptions of *rūpa*, with the vanishing of perceptions of resistance, through non-attention to perceptions of diversity, thinking, ‘Space is boundless,’ enters and dwells in the dimension of boundless space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*].
mn64:13.2: Whatever there is there that is included in *vedanā*, included in *saññā*, included in *saṅkhāra*, included in consciousness [*viññāṇagataṁ*] …
mn64:13.4: not liable to return from that world.
mn64:13.5: This too, Ānanda, is the path, this is the practice, for the abandoning of the five lower fetters.
mn64:14.1: Furthermore, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, having completely transcended the dimension of boundless space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘Consciousness [*viññāṇa*] is boundless,’ enters and dwells in the dimension of boundless consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*].
mn64:14.2: Whatever there is there that is included in *vedanā*, included in *saññā*, included in *saṅkhāra*, included in consciousness [*viññāṇagataṁ*] …
mn64:14.4: not liable to return from that world.
mn64:14.5: This too, Ānanda, is the path, this is the practice, for the abandoning of the five lower fetters.
mn64:15.1: Furthermore, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, having completely transcended the dimension of boundless consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘There is nothing,’ enters and dwells in the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*].
mn64:15.2: Whatever there is there that is included in *vedanā*, included in *saññā*, included in *saṅkhāra*, included in consciousness [*viññāṇagataṁ*] …
mn64:15.4: not liable to return from that world.
mn64:15.5: This too, Ānanda, is the path, this is the practice, for the abandoning of the five lower fetters.”
mn64:16.1: “If this, *bhante*, is the path, this is the practice, for the abandoning of the five lower fetters, then why are some *bhikkhus* liberated in mind and some *bhikkhus* liberated by discernment [*paññā*]?”
mn64:16.2: “Here, Ānanda, I say there is diversity of *indriyas* [*indriyavemattataṁ*].”
mn64:16.4: Satisfied, the Venerable Ānanda delighted in what the *Bhagavā* said.
mn64:16.5: The Greater Mālukya is finished, the fourth.
mn64:2.1: “Do you remember, *bhikkhus*, the five lower fetters that I have taught?”
mn64:2.10: That is how I remember, *bhante*, the five lower fetters taught by the *Bhagavā*.”
mn64:2.2: When this was said, the Venerable Mālukyaputta said to the *Bhagavā*:
mn64:2.3: “I do remember, *bhante*, the five lower fetters that have been taught by the *Bhagavā*.”
mn64:2.4: “But how, Mālukyaputta, do you remember the five lower fetters that have been taught by me?”
mn64:2.5: “I remember, *bhante*, that view of self-identification is taught by the *Bhagavā* as a lower fetter;
mn64:2.6: I remember, *bhante*, that doubt is taught by the *Bhagavā* as a lower fetter;
mn64:2.7: I remember, *bhante*, that attachment to rules and observances is taught by the *Bhagavā* as a lower fetter;
mn64:2.8: I remember, *bhante*, that sensual desire is taught by the *Bhagavā* as a lower fetter;
mn64:2.9: I remember, *bhante*, that ill will is taught by the *Bhagavā* as a lower fetter.
mn64:3.1: “In relation to whom, Mālukyaputta, do you remember these as the five lower fetters thus taught?
mn64:3.10: But the underlying tendency [*anusaya*] to sensual passion lies within him [*anusetvevassa kāmarāgānusayo*].
mn64:3.11: For in a little infant boy, Mālukyaputta, weak and lying on his back, there are not even beings; so how could ill will toward beings arise in him?
mn64:3.12: But the underlying tendency [*anusaya*] to ill will lies within him [*anusetvevassa byāpādānusayo*].
mn64:3.13: Would not wanderers of other sects reproach you with this childish line of reproach?”
mn64:3.2: Would not wanderers of other sects reproach you with this childish line of reproach?
mn64:3.3: For in a little infant boy, Mālukyaputta, weak and lying on his back, there is not even self-identification; so how could view of self-identification arise in him?
mn64:3.4: But the underlying tendency [*anusaya*] to view of self-identification lies within him [*anusetvevassa sakkāyadiṭṭhānusayo*].
mn64:3.5: For in a little infant boy, Mālukyaputta, weak and lying on his back, there are not even *dhammas*; so how could doubt concerning *dhammas* arise in him?
mn64:3.6: But the underlying tendency [*anusaya*] to doubt lies within him [*anusetvevassa vicikicchānusayo*].
mn64:3.7: For in a little infant boy, Mālukyaputta, weak and lying on his back, there is not even virtue; so how could attachment to rules and observances concerning virtues arise in him?
mn64:3.8: But the underlying tendency [*anusaya*] to attachment to rules and observances lies within him [*anusetvevassa sīlabbataparāmāsānusayo*].
mn64:3.9: For in a little infant boy, Mālukyaputta, weak and lying on his back, there are not even sensual pleasures; so how could sensual desire concerning sensual pleasures arise in him?
mn64:4.1: When this was said, the Venerable Ānanda said to the *Bhagavā*:
mn64:4.2: “This is the time, *Bhagavā*, this is the time, Sugata,
mn64:4.3: for the *Bhagavā* to teach the five lower fetters. Having heard it from the *Bhagavā*, the *bhikkhus* will remember it.”
mn64:4.4: “Then listen, Ānanda, and attend appropriately [*yoniso manasi karohi*]. I will speak.”
mn64:4.5: “Yes, *bhante*,” the Venerable Ānanda replied to the *Bhagavā*.
mn64:5.1: “Here, Ānanda, an uninstructed ordinary person, who has not seen the noble ones, is unskilled in the noble *dhamma*, untrained in the noble *dhamma*; who has not seen good persons, is unskilled in the *dhamma* of good persons, untrained in the *dhamma* of good persons—
mn64:5.10: That attachment to rules and observances of his has become entrenched, not thoroughly removed: it is a lower fetter.
mn64:5.11: He dwells with a mind [*citta*] obsessed by sensual passion, overcome by sensual passion;
mn64:5.12: and he does not truly understand the escape from arisen sensual passion as it actually is.
mn64:5.13: That sensual passion of his has become entrenched, not thoroughly removed: it is a lower fetter.
mn64:5.14: He dwells with a mind [*citta*] obsessed by ill will, overcome by ill will;
mn64:5.15: and he does not truly understand the escape from arisen ill will as it actually is.
mn64:5.16: That ill will of his has become entrenched, not thoroughly removed: it is a lower fetter.
mn64:5.2: dwells with a mind [*citta*] obsessed by view of self-identification, overcome by view of self-identification;
mn64:5.3: and he does not truly understand the escape from arisen view of self-identification as it actually is.
mn64:5.4: That view of self-identification of his has become entrenched, not thoroughly removed: it is a lower fetter.
mn64:5.5: He dwells with a mind [*citta*] obsessed by doubt, overcome by doubt;
mn64:5.6: and he does not truly understand the escape from arisen doubt as it actually is.
mn64:5.7: That doubt of his has become entrenched, not thoroughly removed: it is a lower fetter.
mn64:5.8: He dwells with a mind [*citta*] obsessed by attachment to rules and observances, overcome by attachment to rules and observances;
mn64:5.9: and he does not truly understand the escape from arisen attachment to rules and observances as it actually is.
mn64:6.1: But, Ānanda, an instructed noble disciple, who has seen the noble ones, is skilled in the noble *dhamma*, well trained in the noble *dhamma*; who has seen good persons, is skilled in the *dhamma* of good persons, well trained in the *dhamma* of good persons, does not dwell with a mind [*citta*] obsessed by view of self-identification, does not dwell overcome by view of self-identification;
mn64:6.10: He does not dwell with a mind [*citta*] obsessed by sensual passion, does not dwell overcome by sensual passion;
mn64:6.11: and he truly understands the escape from arisen sensual passion as it actually is.
mn64:6.12: That sensual passion is abandoned together with its underlying tendency [*sānusayo*].
mn64:6.13: He does not dwell with a mind [*citta*] obsessed by ill will, does not dwell overcome by ill will;
mn64:6.14: and he truly understands the escape from arisen ill will as it actually is.
mn64:6.15: That ill will is abandoned together with its underlying tendency [*sānusayo*].
mn64:6.16: Whatever path and practice there is, Ānanda, for the abandoning of the five lower fetters—without following that path and that practice, that one would know or see or abandon the five lower fetters: this is impossible.
mn64:6.17: Suppose, Ānanda, there were a great standing tree, full of heartwood, and one might cut through the heartwood without cutting through the bark and without cutting through the sapwood—
mn64:6.19: In the same way, Ānanda, whatever path and practice there is for the abandoning of the five lower fetters—without following that path and that practice, that one would know or see or abandon the five lower fetters: this is impossible.
mn64:6.2: and he truly understands the escape from arisen view of self-identification as it actually is.
mn64:6.3: That view of self-identification is abandoned together with its underlying tendency [*sānusayā*].
mn64:6.4: He does not dwell with a mind [*citta*] obsessed by doubt, does not dwell overcome by doubt;
mn64:6.5: and he truly understands the escape from arisen doubt as it actually is.
mn64:6.6: That doubt is abandoned together with its underlying tendency [*sānusayā*].
mn64:6.7: He does not dwell with a mind [*citta*] obsessed by attachment to rules and observances, does not dwell overcome by attachment to rules and observances;
mn64:6.8: and he truly understands the escape from arisen attachment to rules and observances as it actually is.
mn64:6.9: That attachment to rules and observances is abandoned together with its underlying tendency [*sānusayo*].
mn64:7.1: And whatever path and practice there is, Ānanda, for the abandoning of the five lower fetters—by following that path and that practice, that one would know or see or abandon the five lower fetters: this is possible.
mn64:8.1: Suppose, Ānanda, there were a great standing tree, full of heartwood, and by cutting through the bark and cutting through the sapwood, there would be a cutting through of the heartwood: this is possible.
mn64:8.10: Then a strong man might come and think:
mn64:8.11: ‘By crossing this river Ganges sideways, cutting through the current with my arms, I will go safely to the far shore.’
mn64:8.12: He would be able, by crossing the river Ganges sideways, cutting through the current with his arms, to go safely to the far shore.
mn64:8.13: In the same way, Ānanda, when *dhammas* for the ending of self-identification are being taught, those whose mind [*citta*] leaps forward, becomes confident, settles, becomes liberated—
mn64:8.14: they should be regarded as like that strong man.
mn64:8.2: In the same way, Ānanda, whatever path and practice there is for the abandoning of the five lower fetters—by following that path and that practice, that one would know or see or abandon the five lower fetters: this is possible.
mn64:8.3: Suppose, Ānanda, the river Ganges were full of water, filled to the brim, so that a crow could drink from it.
mn64:8.4: Then a weak man might come and think:
mn64:8.5: ‘By crossing this river Ganges sideways, cutting through the current with my arms, I will go safely to the far shore.’
mn64:8.6: He would not be able, by crossing the river Ganges sideways, cutting through the current with his arms, to go safely to the far shore.
mn64:8.7: In the same way, Ānanda, when *dhammas* for the ending of self-identification are being taught, those whose mind [*citta*] does not leap forward, does not become confident, does not settle, does not become liberated—
mn64:8.8: they should be regarded as like that weak man.
mn64:8.9: Suppose, Ānanda, the river Ganges were full of water, filled to the brim, so that a crow could drink from it.
mn64:9.1: And what, Ānanda, is the path, what is the practice, for the abandoning of the five lower fetters?
mn64:9.2: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, through seclusion [*viveka*] from acquisitions, through the abandoning of unskillful *dhammas*, through the complete calming of all bodily coarseness, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful *dhammas*, enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*].
mn64:9.3: Whatever there is there that is included in *rūpa*, included in *vedanā*, included in *saññā*, included in *saṅkhāra*, included in consciousness [*viññāṇagataṁ*], he contemplates those *dhammas* as impermanent [*aniccato*], as *dukkha* [*dukkhato*], as a disease, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as an affliction, as other, as disintegrating, as empty, as not-self [*anattato*].
mn64:9.4: He turns his mind [*citta*] away from those *dhammas*.
mn64:9.5: Having turned his mind [*citta*] away from those *dhammas*, he directs it toward the deathless element:
mn64:9.6: ‘This is peaceful, this is sublime—that is, the settling [*samatha*] of all *saṅkhāras*, the relinquishment of all acquisitions, the ending of *taṇhā*, dispassion, cessation, *nibbāna*.’
mn64:9.7: If he remains established there, he reaches the destruction of the *āsavas*.
mn64:9.8: But if he does not reach the destruction of the *āsavas*, then through that very desire for *dhamma*, through that delight in *dhamma*, with the exhaustion of the five lower fetters, he becomes one who is spontaneously reborn; there he attains final *nibbāna*, not liable to return from that world.
mn64:9.9: This too, Ānanda, is the path, this is the practice, for the abandoning of the five lower fetters.
